Charts are the visual building blocks of every Dalgo dashboard and report. A chart draws from a single dataset (a table in your warehouse) and displays that data as a bar chart, line chart, pie chart, number, map, or table. Charts are created independently and then added to one or more dashboards.
Creating a chart — how to build a new chart from a dataset Chart types — when to use each chart type
Select Charts in the left menu. You'll see all charts in your organisation. Use the search bar to find a chart by name. Related: Dashboards · Reports

Dalgo offers six chart types. Choose based on what question your data is answering. Chart type Best for Example question Bar Chart Comparing values across categories How many beneficiaries did each district serve this quarter? Line Chart Showing trends over time How has enrolment changed month by month? Pie Chart Showing proportions of a whole What percentage of respondents chose each answer? Number Highlighting a single key metric Total households reached this year Map Visualising data by geography Which regions have the highest case counts? Table Displaying raw data in rows and columns Full list of beneficiaries with their status

Bar vs Line: Use bar for category comparisons; use line when the x-axis is time. Pie charts: Work best with 5 or fewer slices. More than that becomes hard to read. Number: Use for KPIs on a monitoring dashboard. Combine with a Line chart below it to show the trend behind the number. Map: Requires a geographic column (country, state, or coordinates) in your dataset. Next: Creating a chart · Dashboards
Charts are created from a dataset — a table in your warehouse — and then added to dashboards.

Select Charts in the left menu. Select + New Chart. Choose a chart type. See Chart types for guidance on which to use. Select the dataset (the warehouse table) your chart will draw from. Select Configure. In the configuration screen, choose which columns to use for axes, values, filters, and labels. Give your chart a name. Select Save. The chart is now saved and available to add to any dashboard.

The right-hand Data panel has a Metrics section where you define what the chart measures. Select + Add another metric to open the metric form. The form has three tabs: Simple — pick an aggregation (sum, avg, count, etc.) and a column from the dataset. Calculated — write a SQL expression that returns a single number. Saved — pick from your Metrics library. To use an existing metric, switch to the Saved tab and select one from the dropdown labelled Select a metric from pre-defined list. The chart now shows the saved metric's definition, and any future edit to the metric flows through to this chart automatically. You can mix saved and ad-hoc metrics in the same chart — bar, line, and table charts accept multiple metrics, while pie, number, and map charts use one. info A small library icon next to a metric's label means it is a saved metric. Hover over it to see the metric name. If you build a metric in Simple or Calculated mode and want to reuse it elsewhere, expand the Add metric to library section in the metric form and select Add metric to library — the metric is saved and added to the chart in one step.
Select Charts in the left menu. Find the chart you want to edit and select the ⋯ menu on its row. Select Edit. Make your changes and select Save.

Select Charts in the left menu. Select the ⋯ menu on the chart row. Select Delete. warning Deleting a chart removes it from all dashboards that use it. This cannot be undone. Next: Chart types · Adding charts to a dashboard

Key terms used throughout Dalgo and these docs, defined in plain language. Warehouse The central database where all your data is stored after it has been ingested. Dalgo supports BigQuery and PostgreSQL. Think of it as the single source of truth your charts and dashboards draw from. Source Any external system where your data lives before it comes into Dalgo. Common examples: KoboToolbox, Google Sheets, CommCare, Avni, Salesforce, Zoho. You add a source once by saving its credentials. Connection A link between a source and your warehouse. A connection tells Dalgo which data streams (tables) to sync, how often to sync them, and how to handle updates. One source can have multiple connections. Sync The act of copying data from a source into your warehouse via a connection. Syncs run automatically on a schedule or can be triggered manually. Pipeline A scheduled job that runs one or more connections (to sync data) and optionally runs transformation tasks afterwards. Pipelines are configured in Orchestrate. Transform / dbt After raw data lands in your warehouse, it usually needs cleaning, joining, and computing before it's useful. Dalgo uses dbt (data build tool) to run SQL-based transformations. You don't need to know SQL to use the UI Transform canvas, but dbt is what runs underneath. Model A single transformation step in dbt — typically one SQL file that produces one table. Dalgo's UI Transform canvas lets you create models visually. Chart A single visualisation — a bar chart, pie chart, line chart, number, map, or table — built from one dataset (warehouse table). Charts are the building blocks of dashboards. Dashboard A collection of charts arranged on a canvas. Dashboards always show live data — they update automatically each time your pipeline runs. Report A frozen snapshot of a dashboard at a specific date range. Unlike dashboards, reports don't update. They're used for quarterly reviews, donor updates, and team discussions. Pipeline Overview The monitoring screen inside the Data section that shows a visual history of every pipeline run — when it ran, how long it took, and whether it succeeded. Elementary An open-source dbt observability package that Dalgo uses for data quality monitoring. Once set up, it surfaces test results, anomalies, and model run history. Superset An open-source BI tool that Dalgo optionally hosts for organisations on the Dalgo + Superset plan. It offers more advanced dashboarding than Dalgo's native dashboards.

Dashboards arrange charts into shareable views that always show your latest data. You can create multiple dashboards for different audiences — a field team view, a donor summary, a management overview. Each dashboard is made up of charts you've already created in the Charts section.

Page What it covers Viewing dashboards Browse and open existing dashboards Creating a dashboard Build a new dashboard, add charts, and set your landing page Superset Usage Platform usage analytics (Superset subscription required) Superset Advanced dashboarding via hosted Superset (Superset subscription required) Related: Charts · Reports · Impact
Build a new dashboard by combining existing charts into a single view.

Select Dashboards in the left menu. Select + Create Dashboard. Dalgo immediately creates a new empty dashboard and opens the builder. In the builder, select + Add Chart to browse and add existing charts to the canvas. Drag charts to reposition them. Drag the edges or corners to resize. Select Save when you are done. info Dalgo creates a draft dashboard the moment you select + Create Dashboard. If you leave without saving, the draft is kept — you can continue editing it from the dashboard list.
Select Dashboards in the left menu. Open the dashboard you want to edit. Select Edit (top-right of the dashboard). Add, remove, or rearrange charts as needed. Select Save when you are done.

Any dashboard can be set as your personal landing screen, shown when you open the Impact home. Open the dashboard you want to use. Select Set as my landing page from the dashboard menu (top-right). note This setting is personal — it only affects your account. Other users keep their own landing page. Next: Superset Usage · Charts

documents.t: Superset is an optional hosted dashboarding environment within Dalgo for organisations that need more flexible, role-based visualisations. note Superset is only available to organisations on the Dalgo + Superset plan. Contact support@dalgo.org to add it to your subscription.

Select Dashboards in the left menu. Select the Open Superset option. A sign-in prompt will appear. Select Sign in with Google. If your email has been granted access, you will be logged in and Superset will open. Screenshot coming soon A screenshot of the Superset sign-in prompt will be added here. Once logged in, your warehouse data is already connected. You can immediately start building charts, dashboards, and applying role-based access controls inside Superset. note If your email has not been granted access to Superset, contact your organisation's Superset administrator or reach out to support@dalgo.org.
For help building charts and dashboards inside Superset, refer to the official Superset documentation. Dalgo does not duplicate Superset's own docs. Related: Dashboards · Superset Usage

The Superset Usage dashboard shows how your organisation is using Dalgo — which dashboards are being viewed, by whom, and how often. note The Superset Usage dashboard requires a Dalgo + Superset subscription. If you see an error when opening this page, your organisation may not have Superset enabled. Contact support@dalgo.org for more information.
Select Dashboards in the left menu. Select Superset Usage. The dashboard loads automatically as an embedded Superset view.

The Metrics tab shows: Active Users — a bar graph of active and inactive users. A user is counted as active if they have visited at least one dashboard. List of users — all users in your organisation with their total dashboard visits. List of dashboards — all dashboards with their total visit count. Filters at the top let you view metrics by month, role, and dashboard.

The Trends tab shows: Number of users accessing dashboards — a monthly trendline of unique dashboard viewers, with change vs. the previous month. Visits per user — a monthly trendline of the average visits per user, with change vs. the previous month. Top-level filters let you slice trends by role and dashboard. Related: Dashboards · Superset
Browse and open the dashboards available to your organisation.

Select Dashboards in the left menu. You'll see all dashboards in your organisation. Use the search bar to find a dashboard by name. Select a dashboard name to open it. The dashboard displays all its charts using live data from your warehouse. info Dashboards always show your latest data. If you need a frozen snapshot for a specific date range, use Reports instead. Next: Creating a dashboard · Superset Usage

The Data section is where you connect sources, transform raw data, schedule pipelines, explore your warehouse, and monitor data quality. This section maps to the Data item in the Dalgo left navigation and covers everything behind the scenes that keeps your dashboards and reports up to date.

Page What it covers Overview Monitor pipeline run history and health Ingest Connect your warehouse, sources, and set up sync connections Transform Clean, join, and compute your data using dbt Orchestrate Schedule pipelines to run automatically Explore Browse the tables in your warehouse Data Quality Monitor dbt test results and data anomalies Related: Dashboards · Reports

Explore lets you browse and query the tables in your data warehouse directly inside Dalgo — no SQL client needed. Screenshot coming soon Screenshots for the Explore page will be added in a future update.
Select Data in the left menu. Select the Explore tab. You will see a list of schemas and tables in your connected warehouse.
Select a schema to expand it and see its tables. Select a table to preview its columns and recent rows.

Open a table. Use the query editor to write a SQL query against the table. Select Run to execute. Results appear in a table below the editor. note Explore is a read-only view. You can query and inspect data but cannot modify tables or run write operations. Related: Ingest · Transform · Data Quality

Ingest is where you connect your data warehouse and sources so Dalgo can automatically pull your data into one place. The Ingest section has three tabs. If you're setting up for the first time, work through them in this order: Connections → Sources → Warehouse (or start with Warehouse if it isn't set up yet). Tab What it covers Connections Link a source to your warehouse to start syncing data (shown by default) Sources Add the external systems your data comes from Warehouse Connect the database where your data will be stored note You may need to whitelist these IP addresses in your organisation's firewall before Dalgo can connect to your data sources: 13.202.128.47 65.2.173.97 Related: Transform · Orchestrate

A connection tells Dalgo what data to sync from a saved source into your warehouse. If a source answers "where does the data live?", a connection answers "which data should be copied into the warehouse, and how should it sync?".
Select Data in the left menu, then select Ingest. The Connections tab is shown by default. You will see a list of all connections in your organisation.

Select + Add Connection. Give your connection a name and select the source you want to sync. The available tables or data streams from that source will appear. Select which streams (tables) you want to sync. Choose the sync mode for each stream. Select Connect to save. The first sync will start automatically. To test your connection, select the Sync button on the connection row. Sync logs will appear below.

From each connection row you can: Sync now — trigger an immediate sync using the sync icon View history — see past sync runs and their status using the history icon Edit — change stream selection or settings using the ⋯ menu Delete — remove the connection using the ⋯ menu warning Deleting a connection is permanent and removes all its sync history. This cannot be undone.

If columns or fields change in your source data, Dalgo detects those changes automatically. When a change is detected, a Pending Changes section appears in the Connections tab listing the affected connections. Select View next to a connection to review what changed. You can approve or ignore the changes. Approving updates the connection so future syncs use the new schema. note If a critical field (such as a cursor field) is removed, Dalgo treats it as a breaking change that cannot be approved in Dalgo. The issue must be fixed at the source first. Next: Sources · Transform

A source is any system where your data already lives. Common examples include KoboToolbox, Google Sheets, CommCare, Avni, Zoho, and many others. When you add a source in Dalgo, you are saving the access credentials for that system. After that, you create a connection to choose which data syncs into your warehouse.
Select Data in the left menu, then select Ingest. Select the Sources tab. You will see all sources added to your organisation. Use the search bar to find a source by name or type.

Select + Add Source. A dialog will appear. Search for or select the Source Type (e.g. KoboToolbox, Google Sheets). Enter a Source Name. Fill in the required credentials or connection details. The fields shown depend on the source type. If you are unsure what credentials are needed, check the provider's documentation or contact the person who manages that system. Select Save Changes and Test. Dalgo will confirm it can reach the source. Select Save once the test succeeds.

On the Sources tab, select the ⋯ menu on the source row. Select Edit to update credentials and test again, or Delete to remove the source. note You cannot change the source type of an existing source. If you need a different source type, create a new source instead. warning Deleting a source also removes any connections that depend on it. This cannot be undone. Next: Connections · Warehouse

Your warehouse is the single database where data from all your sources is stored after syncing. Dalgo supports BigQuery and PostgreSQL as warehouse destinations. You need to set up your warehouse before adding sources or connections.

Select Data in the left menu, then select Ingest. Select the Your Warehouse tab. Select the warehouse type from the Destination type dropdown — either BigQuery or Postgres. Enter a name in the Name field. Fill in the connection details: BigQuery — requires a project ID and service account JSON credentials Postgres — requires a host, port, database name, username, and password Select Test Connection. Dalgo will verify it can reach your warehouse. Select Save once the test succeeds. Screenshot coming soon A screenshot showing a successful warehouse connection test will be added here.

Select the Your Warehouse tab. Select Edit. Make your changes, test the connection, and select Save. note If you need help obtaining warehouse credentials, contact your internal tech team, your implementation partner, or the Dalgo team at support@dalgo.org. Next: Sources · Connections

Metrics are reusable measurements — a saved combination of a column and an aggregation — that power your KPIs and charts. Defining a measurement once and reusing it everywhere keeps numbers consistent across your team. When the definition of "Active beneficiaries" lives in the Metrics library, every chart, KPI, and report that references it stays in sync.

Select Data in the left menu, then select Metrics. The library lists every metric defined for your organisation, with columns for: Name — the metric name and a short description. Mode — Simple (column + aggregation) or Calculated (SQL expression). Data source — the schema and table the metric reads from. Expression — a preview of how the value is computed (e.g. SUM(amount) or COUNT(*)). Used by — the number of charts and KPIs that reference this metric. Select the count to see a list. Last updated — when the definition last changed. Use the Name column header to filter or sort, and the Data source and Last updated headers to sort.

Select + Create Metric in the top-right. Enter a Name — this is what your team will see in chart and KPI pickers. Add an optional Definition describing what the metric measures. Pick a Data source — the warehouse schema and table the metric reads from. Choose a mode:

Pick a built-in aggregation and a column. Function — sum, avg, count, min, max, or count_distinct. Column — the column to aggregate. For count, the column auto-fills to * (count all rows). For numeric aggregations (sum, avg, min, max), only numeric columns are listed.

Write a SQL expression that returns a single numeric value — useful for ratios, weighted averages, or anything Simple mode can't express. Example: SUM(amount_paid) / NULLIF(SUM(amount_due), 0) for collection rate. The expression runs against your warehouse when you save. You'll see Expression valid if it works, or the database error if it doesn't. Select Save to add the metric to the library.

Select the metric name or use the ⋯ menu and choose Edit Metric. warning If the metric is already used by charts or KPIs, editing its definition changes what those visuals show. The edit dialog shows you which charts and KPIs depend on this metric before you save.
Use the ⋯ menu on the row and select Delete. Deletion is blocked while charts or KPIs still reference the metric — remove those dependencies first.

From any metric row, use the ⋯ menu and select Create KPI. The KPI form opens with the metric pre-selected — you only need to add a target, RAG thresholds, and a time grain. See Creating a KPI. Next: KPIs · Charts · Explore

documents.t: Orchestrate lets you schedule your data pipeline to run automatically — combining sync connections and transformation tasks into a single job. A pipeline combines one or more of your data connections with transformation tasks, then runs them automatically on a schedule you choose. You can also run a pipeline manually at any time.

Select Data in the left menu, then select Orchestrate. You will see a list of your existing pipelines. Each row shows: Pipeline — the name of the pipeline Schedule — how often the pipeline runs and your local timezone Status — whether the pipeline is Active or Inactive Last Run — when the pipeline last ran and who triggered it Result — the outcome of the last run (Success, Failed, Running, etc.) Actions — buttons to view history, run, or access more options

Select Create Pipeline in the top-right corner. Enter a Name for your pipeline. In the Connections field, select one or more connections to sync. Under Transform Tasks, check Run transform tasks if you want dbt to run after syncing. A task list will appear — you can reorder or remove tasks. info The following tasks run automatically before your transformation tasks — you don't need to add them manually: Git pull/clone — pulls the latest code from your Git repository dbt clean — removes compiled dbt artifacts dbt deps — installs dbt package dependencies

The Schedule section is on the right side of the pipeline form. Under Frequency, choose: Daily — runs every day at a time you set Weekly — runs on specific days at a time you set Manual — only runs when you trigger it yourself If you chose Weekly, select the Days of the Week. If you chose Daily or Weekly, set the Time of Day (shown in your local timezone). Select Create Pipeline to save.

On the pipeline list, select the run icon (↻) on the pipeline row to trigger a run immediately. note You cannot trigger a new run while a pipeline is already running. The run button will be disabled until the current run finishes.

Select the ⋯ menu on the pipeline row. Select Edit. Update any fields — name, connections, transform tasks, or schedule. Use the Is Active? toggle to pause or resume the automatic schedule without deleting the pipeline. Select Save Changes.

Select the history icon (clock) on a pipeline row. A panel opens showing all past runs. Each run shows the date, who triggered it, and the outcome. Select a task row to expand and view its detailed logs.

Select the ⋯ menu on the pipeline row. Select Delete. warning Deleting a pipeline is permanent. Its schedule, configuration, and run history will all be removed and cannot be recovered. Related: Ingest → Connections · Transform · Overview

The Overview shows a visual history of your data pipeline runs so you can quickly see what ran, when, and whether it succeeded. This is a read-only monitoring view. To manage or schedule pipelines, go to Orchestrate.

Select Data in the left menu, then select the Overview tab. You will see a card for each pipeline. Each card shows: The pipeline name A bar chart of recent runs — green bars are successful, red bars are failed, yellow bars indicate the pipeline ran but a dbt test failed The last run time and who triggered it
Select any bar in the chart to expand the run's logs directly below the pipeline card. You will see each task that ran, its duration, and whether it succeeded or failed.

Each pipeline card has a Scale to runtime toggle. When enabled, bar heights are proportional to how long each run took — useful for spotting runs that were unusually slow. When disabled, all bars are the same height and only colour indicates success or failure. Next: Orchestrate · Data Quality

Dalgo's data quality feature uses Elementary to monitor your dbt models and surface data issues automatically. Elementary is a dbt observability package that runs alongside your transformations. Once set up, it shows test results, anomalies, and model run history across all your data models.

Select Data in the left menu. Select the Quality tab. What you see depends on your setup: If dbt is not configured — you'll see a message asking you to set up your Transform workspace first. Go to Transform to get started. If Elementary is not yet set up — you'll see a one-time setup prompt. Select Set up Elementary to add it to your dbt project. If Elementary is set up — the full report loads automatically.
When you first enable Elementary, Dalgo installs and configures the package in your dbt project.

Once Elementary is running, the Data Quality page shows: Test results — pass/fail status for each dbt test across your models Model runs — when each model last ran and whether it succeeded Anomalies — automatically detected irregularities in row counts, freshness, or column values note Results update each time your pipeline runs transformation tasks. If you haven't run a pipeline recently, the report may not show your latest data. If you need help interpreting a failing check, contact your data team or support@dalgo.org. Related: Transform · Orchestrate · Overview

Transform is where raw data from your warehouse is cleaned, joined, and computed into the tables your dashboards and reports draw from. Dalgo runs transformations using dbt (data build tool). When you first visit Transform, Dalgo automatically creates a managed dbt workspace for your organisation — a private Git repository and a scaffolded dbt project. Once your workspace is ready, you'll see two tabs: Tab Who it's for UI Transform Anyone — build dbt models visually without writing SQL DBT Transform Data practitioners — run dbt commands directly and manage tasks You can also connect your own GitHub repository instead of using the Dalgo-managed one.

UI Transform — visual canvas for building models DBT Transform — task runner and direct dbt command access Switching repositories — bring your own GitHub repo Related: Ingest · Orchestrate · Data Quality

DBT Transform gives you direct access to dbt commands — run tasks, view logs, and create custom dbt operations. This tab is intended for users who work directly with dbt. If you're building models visually, see UI Transform.
Select Data in the left menu, then select Transform. Select the DBT Transform tab.

At the top of both tabs you'll see the DBT Repository card showing: The connected Git repository URL The default schema (e.g. intermediate) An info icon (for managed repos) indicating the repository is created and managed by Dalgo note If you need access to the managed Git repository, reach out on Discord.

The task list shows all available dbt tasks: dbt-run — executes your dbt models dbt-test — runs your dbt tests dbt-deps — installs dbt package dependencies dbt-docs-generate — generates dbt documentation Select the Run (▶) button next to any task to execute it. Logs appear inline below the task.

Select + NEW TASK in the top-right corner of the DBT Actions card. Select a task type from the dropdown. Configure any flags or options. Review the command preview. Select Create. Custom tasks appear in your task list and can be deleted when no longer needed. Next: Switching repositories · Orchestrate

By default, Dalgo manages the Git repository for your dbt project. You can switch to your own GitHub repository at any time. note This page is primarily for implementation partners and technical teams setting up Dalgo for an organisation.

Select EDIT on the DBT Repository card. Enter your GitHub repo URL (e.g. https://github.com/your-org/your-dbt-repo). Enter a Personal access token with the following permissions: repo (full control of private repositories) workflow (update GitHub Action workflows) Set the Dbt default Schema for your dbt project. Select SAVE & UPDATE.

If your external repo is empty: Dalgo copies your existing models from the managed repository into your external repository — no work is lost. If your external repo already has a dbt project: Dalgo uses the existing project from your external repository. warning Any models created in the managed workspace will not be carried over when switching to a non-empty external repository. After switching, you manage the repository yourself (commits, branches, pull requests, etc.).

You can also switch from one external repository to another using the same Edit flow. The new repository is always cloned fresh. warning Models from the previous repository are not migrated to the new one. Model migration is only supported when switching from a Dalgo-managed repository to an empty external repository. Related: DBT Transform · Transform overview
UI Transform provides a visual canvas for building dbt models without writing SQL. Use it to create and connect data models by dragging and dropping operations — no code required.
Select Data in the left menu, then select Transform. Select the UI Transform tab.
Select Edit Workflow to open the full canvas editor. In the editor, drag and drop operations to build your models. Connect operations by linking their outputs to inputs.

When you are happy with your changes: Select Publish. Dalgo commits and pushes your changes to the connected Git repository. The next pipeline run will use the updated models. info Publishing writes directly to the default branch of your managed repository. If you need a review process, consider switching to your own GitHub repository so you can use pull requests. Next: DBT Transform · Switching repositories
Impact at a Glance is the first screen you see when you log in — your personal home screen showing the dashboard your team has set as the default view.

When you open Dalgo, you land here automatically. If a default dashboard has been configured for your account, it loads straight away and you can read charts, apply filters, and explore data without navigating elsewhere. If no landing page has been set yet, you'll see two options: Choose a dashboard — select an existing dashboard to display here Create new dashboard — build a new dashboard from scratch and set it as your home

Select Dashboards in the left menu. Open the dashboard you want to use as your home screen. Select Set as my landing page from the dashboard menu (top-right corner). The next time you log in, that dashboard appears on the Impact screen automatically. info Each user sets their own landing page independently. Changing yours has no effect on what other team members see when they log in. Next: Charts · Dashboards · Reports

Dalgo is an open-source data platform that helps NGOs automate data collection, transformation, and visualisation — without needing a data engineering team. Your data lives in many places: KoboToolbox surveys, Google Sheets, CommCare, Avni, Zoho, and more. Dalgo pulls it all into one warehouse, cleans and joins it using dbt, runs everything on a schedule, and surfaces the results as charts, dashboards, and reports — all through a single interface. Visit dalgo.org to learn more about pricing, or reach out at support@dalgo.org. Join our Discord server to chat with the team and the community.

The left navigation mirrors your workflow: Section What it does Impact Your home screen — shows your default dashboard at a glance Charts Create and manage the visual building blocks of your dashboards Dashboards Arrange charts into shareable views Reports Capture point-in-time snapshots of dashboards to share or review Data Ingest, transform, orchestrate, explore, and monitor your data Settings Manage users, billing, and organisation settings

If your pipelines haven't been set up yet, start with the Quickstart → — a short linear walkthrough from login to your first report. If your Dalgo workspace is already running and you just need to look something up, jump straight to the relevant section in the sidebar. note Superset dashboards and the Usage Dashboard require a Dalgo + Superset subscription. Features behind a subscription are marked throughout these docs.

KPIs (Key Performance Indicators) are the headline numbers your team tracks — each one paired with a target and a status so you can see at a glance whether you are on track. A KPI in Dalgo combines a saved Metric with a target value, a time period, and Red / Amber / Green (RAG) thresholds. The result is a card that shows the current value, how it compares to your target, the trend over time, and a status badge. KPIs are the leadership-facing layer on top of your data. Use them for the indicators you review in team meetings, share with funders, or publish on a dashboard.

Select KPIs in the left menu to open the KPI library. Each card shows: The KPI name and program tag. A status badge — On Track, Needs Attention, or Off Track. The current value and the target. A period-over-period change — for example, ↑ +12.3% from last month. A small trend chart showing recent periods. The date the underlying data was last refreshed.

Use the controls above the card grid to narrow the list: Search KPIs — find a KPI by name. Type — filter by KPI classification (Input, Output, Outcome, Impact). Program — filter by program tag. Status — show only On Track, Needs Attention, or Off Track KPIs.

Select the ⋯ menu on any card to: View KPI — open the drilldown drawer with a larger chart and notes timeline. Edit KPI — change the name, target, RAG thresholds, tags, or classification. Delete — remove the KPI (blocked if it is on a dashboard — see Deleting a KPI). Download as PNG — save the card as an image. Export Data as CSV — download the trend data.

Select the ⋯ menu on the KPI card. Select Delete. Confirm in the dialog. If the KPI is used on one or more dashboards, deletion is blocked. The dialog lists every dashboard that uses the KPI — remove it from those dashboards first, then delete. warning Deleting a KPI is permanent. The KPI definition, its target, and any notes you added are removed. Next: Creating a KPI · Notes and drilldown · Metrics
Every KPI is built on top of a saved Metric. Before you create a KPI, make sure the underlying measurement exists in your Metrics library.
Select KPIs in the left menu. Select + Create KPI (or Create your first KPI if the library is empty).

Open the Select metric dropdown and pick a metric from your Metrics Library. Each entry shows the metric name, its data source, and whether it is a Simple or Calculated metric. The Name this KPI field auto-fills with the metric name. Edit it if you want a different label on the card. Enter a Target Value — the number you want this KPI to reach. Choose a Direction: Higher is better — the KPI should increase toward the target (e.g. students enrolled). Lower is better — the KPI should decrease toward the target (e.g. dropout rate). Select Continue. note If the metric you need doesn't exist yet, select + Create a new metric at the bottom of the metric dropdown to open the Metrics library in a new tab.

This step controls how the KPI is colour-coded and what time grain it tracks. Target & RAG status Set the thresholds for each status as a percentage of your target. The card colour and badge update live as values move across these bands. On Track (green) — defaults to 100% or more of target. Needs Attention (amber) — defaults to 80% or more of target (or 120% or less, if direction is "Lower is better"). Off Track (red) — anything outside the amber band; calculated automatically. The form shows the absolute value next to each percentage so you can sanity-check the bands. Time configuration Time column — the date or timestamp column from the metric's table that periods are bucketed by. Time grain — Daily, Weekly, Monthly, Quarterly, or Yearly. The trend chart and period-over-period change use this grain. Classification (optional) Program tags — type a tag and press Enter or comma to add it. Existing tags appear as suggestions. Use these to group KPIs by program in the filter bar. KPI type — pick one of Input, Output, Outcome, or Impact. Use these to follow your team's results framework. Select Create KPI to save. The new KPI appears on the KPI page with a status badge and trend chart, once the underlying metric has data for the chosen time column.
documents.s: Step 2 — RAG status, time period, classification

documents.t: Select the ⋯ menu on the KPI card and choose Edit KPI. Update any field and select Save KPI. note The linked metric, time column, and time grain cannot be changed after a KPI is created. To change any of these, delete the KPI and create a new one. Editing the target value recolours historical RAG status — the trend chart re-evaluates every period against the new thresholds. Next: Notes and drilldown · KPIs overview · Metrics

Open a KPI card to see a larger trend chart and add notes — short comments or beneficiary quotes attached to a specific time period. Notes give context to the numbers. When a KPI dipped in March because a flood disrupted field work, write that down on the March period. Reviewers see the note next to the value and don't have to ask.

Select KPIs in the left menu. Select a KPI card. The drawer opens from the right of the screen and shows: The KPI name and a link back to the underlying metric. A larger trend chart with the same RAG colours. The current value, target, and period-over-period change. A Notes timeline below the chart.

Inside the drawer you can adjust how the trend chart is displayed without changing the KPI definition itself: Date range — pick a custom range or a preset such as Last 90 days or Year to date. Time grain — switch between Daily, Weekly, Monthly, Quarterly, or Yearly to zoom in or out. These choices only affect the drawer view. The KPI card on the main page keeps its saved time grain.

Notes are tied to a specific period — for example, "Q2 2025" if the time grain is Quarterly. In the drawer, select + Add Note. Choose the period the note applies to. Pick a note type: Note — general commentary. Beneficiary Quote — a quote from someone the program served. Write the note text and select Save. The note appears in the timeline alongside the value and period-over-period change for that period. info Notes capture a snapshot of the KPI value at the time you write them. If the underlying data is later corrected, the note keeps the original value so you can see what was true when the comment was made.
Use the ⋯ menu on any note to edit or delete it. Deletions are immediate and cannot be undone. Next: KPIs overview · Creating a KPI · Dashboards

Through this step Dalgo enables you to automate your data pipeline by setting up scheduled ingestion and transformation. Select orchestrate on the left menu panel. You will see a list of your existing pipelines with their schedule, status, last run details, and results. Select "+ Create Pipeline" - This will take you to the "Create Pipeline" screen. Give your pipeline a name. Select one or more of the connections you have set up. Configure your Transform Tasks: Check "Run transform tasks" to include transformation tasks in your pipeline. When enabled, default system tasks (like dbt run, dbt test) are pre-populated. You can add custom tasks from the dropdown and reorder them using drag-and-drop. Use "Reset to default" to restore the default task list. info The following tasks are automatically added (in this order) before your transformation tasks and do not need to be configured manually: Git pull/clone — pulls the latest code from the default branch of your git repository. dbt clean — removes compiled dbt artifacts. dbt deps — installs dbt package dependencies. These tasks are not shown in the pipeline transform task dropdown. Set the schedule for your pipeline and click "Create Pipeline". You can edit an existing pipeline by clicking the three-dot menu on a pipeline row and selecting "Edit". You can test your pipeline by clicking the run icon on the pipeline row. You can view the logs history of your past runs by clicking the history icon. This shows each task's duration and lets you view detailed logs.

New to Dalgo? This five-step walkthrough takes you from your invitation email to your first report — in under an hour. Each step is one screen. At the end of each step you'll find a link to the deeper reference page if you want more detail.

Setting up your account — accept your invitation and log in for the first time Your home: Impact at a Glance — understand what you're looking at when you first sign in Viewing your first dashboard — open a dashboard and read its charts Creating your first report — capture a snapshot to share with your team Where to go next — a map of what to explore depending on your role → Ready? Start with Setting up your account
You'll receive an email invitation from notifications@dalgo.org. This page walks you through accepting it and logging in for the first time.

Open the invitation email from notifications@dalgo.org. Select the link in the email. Your browser will open the Dalgo platform. Enter a password and confirm it. Select Accept Invitation. You are now logged in and will land on your Impact at a Glance home screen. note If you don't see the invitation email, check your spam folder. If it still isn't there, ask the person who set up your Dalgo workspace to resend the invitation from Settings → User Management. → Next: Your home: Impact at a Glance
Dashboards are collections of charts. This step shows you how to open one and understand what you're looking at.

Select Dashboards in the left menu. You'll see all dashboards in your organisation. 2. Select any dashboard name to open it. 3. Each tile on the dashboard is a chart. Hover over a chart to see its exact values. 4. If the dashboard has filters at the top, use them to narrow the data by date, region, or other dimensions. info Dashboards always show the latest data — they update automatically each time your data pipeline runs.
If the dashboard list is empty, your implementation partner may not have created any yet. Reach out to them, or contact support@dalgo.org. → Next: Creating your first report → Reference: Dashboards
A report captures a dashboard's charts at a specific point in time — useful for quarterly reviews, donor updates, or team meetings.

Select Reports in the left menu. 2. Select + CREATE REPORT in the top-right corner. 3. Choose a Dashboard to base the report on. 4. Give the report a Name (e.g. "Q1 2025 Program Update"). 5. Select the Date column Dalgo should use to filter data by date. 6. Set the Start date and End date for the period you want to cover. 7. Select Create. The report is saved and appears in your reports list. Select it to open and view the charts, filtered to your chosen date range.
Once the report is open, select the share icon (↗) in the top-right corner to share it via a public link or send it as a PDF by email. → Next: Where to go next → Reference: Reports
When you log in, Dalgo drops you on the Impact at a Glance screen — your personal home screen showing the dashboard your team has chosen as the default view.
documents.s: Your home: Impact at a Glance

documents.t: If a default dashboard has been configured, it loads here automatically. You can read charts, apply filters, and explore data just as you would inside the Dashboards section. If no default dashboard is set yet, you'll see two options: Choose a dashboard — pick an existing dashboard to display here Create new dashboard — build a new one from scratch

You can change which dashboard appears here at any time. Select Dashboards in the left menu. Open the dashboard you want to use as your home screen. Select Set as my landing page from the dashboard menu (top-right). The next time you log in, that dashboard will appear on the Impact screen automatically. info Each user sets their own landing page. Changing yours doesn't affect what other team members see. → Next: Viewing your first dashboard → Reference: Impact
You've logged in, explored a dashboard, and created a report. Here's a map of what to explore next, depending on your role.

I want to… Go to Build a new chart Charts → Creating a chart Build a new dashboard Dashboards → Creating a dashboard Share a report with my team Reports → Sharing reports Check whether my pipeline ran last night Data → Overview

documents.t: → Support — how to get help, troubleshooting steps, and community links.

Reports capture a snapshot of any dashboard at a specific point in time — so you can review, share, and comment on your data. A report freezes your dashboard's layout and charts as they appeared when the report was created. You choose a date range, and the report shows data for that period. Unlike dashboards, which always show the latest data, reports preserve a fixed view — useful for quarterly reviews, donor updates, or team discussions.

Creating a report — build a new report from a dashboard Executive summaries and comments — add context and discuss findings with your team Sharing reports — share via public link or email Exporting and deleting — download as PDF or remove a report

Select Reports in the left menu. You will see a list of all reports in your organisation. Each report shows its Title, the Dashboard Used to create it, who created it, and when. Select the filter icon on any column header to search within that column. Select any column header to sort. Select again to reverse. Related: Dashboards · Charts
Add written context to a report and discuss findings with your team directly on the charts.

The Executive Summary is a free-text section at the top of every report. Use it for key takeaways, observations, or notes for reviewers. Open the report you want to edit. Select the pencil (✎) icon next to the Executive Summary heading. Type your notes in the text area that appears. Select Save to keep your changes, or Cancel to discard them.

You can leave comments on the executive summary or on any individual chart. Select the comment icon on the executive summary or on a chart card. Type your comment in the text field. To mention a team member, type @ followed by their name. They will receive a notification. Select Send to post your comment. To edit or delete one of your own comments, select the options icon next to the comment. note Unread comments are shown with a highlighted comment icon on the chart or summary section. Next: Sharing reports · Exporting and deleting
Create a report from any existing dashboard to capture its charts filtered to a specific date range.

Select Reports in the left menu. Select + CREATE REPORT in the top-right corner. Choose the Dashboard you want to base the report on. Give your report a Name (e.g. "Q1 2025 Program Report"). Select the Date column Dalgo should use to filter data by date range. Set the Start date and End date for the period you want to cover. Select Create. info The report captures your dashboard's layout and chart settings at the time of creation. If you later change the original dashboard, existing reports are not affected.

Select any report row in the list to open it. At the top you'll see the report's title, date range, creator, and a link to the original dashboard. The Executive Summary section appears at the top — a free-text area where you or your team can add notes and key takeaways. Below the summary, all charts from the dashboard are shown, filtered to the report's date range. Next: Executive summaries and comments · Sharing reports
Open the report you want to export. Select the download icon in the top-right corner. The report downloads as a PDF file to your device.

On the reports list, select the ⋯ menu on the report row. Select Delete. warning Deleting a report is permanent. The report and all its comments will be removed and cannot be recovered. Related: Reports overview · Sharing reports
Share a report with anyone — including people outside your organisation — via a public link or by email.
Select the share icon (↗) on the report row in the Actions column, or in the top-right corner when viewing a report. Choose one of the options:

Generates a public URL that anyone with the link can use to view the report — no Dalgo account required. Toggle sharing on to activate the link. Toggle it off to deactivate the link (the URL will stop working). The dialog shows how many times the link has been accessed.

Sends the report as a PDF attachment to one or more email addresses. Enter recipient email addresses, separated by commas. Optionally add a custom subject line. Select Send. Next: Exporting and deleting
Settings is where you manage your organisation's users, billing, and platform configuration.

Page What it covers User Management Invite team members and control their access Billing View your subscription and manage billing details About Platform version and organisation details Related: Support
The About page shows your platform version, organisation details, and links to open-source licence information.
Select Settings in the left menu. Select About.

Platform version — the version of Dalgo your organisation is running Organisation name — your registered organisation name Licence information — Dalgo is open source under the AGPL-3.0 licence Related: Settings
The Billing page shows your organisation's current subscription and lets you manage billing details.
Select Settings in the left menu. Select Billing.
Your current plan and the features it includes Your billing cycle and next renewal date An option to update payment details or contact the Dalgo team about your subscription
To add Superset, increase usage limits, or discuss a custom plan, contact support@dalgo.org. The Dalgo team will update your subscription and confirm the changes. Related: Settings · Support
User Management lets you add team members to your Dalgo organisation and control what each person can see and do.

Select Settings in the left menu, then select User Management. You will see two tabs: Users — all current members of your organisation and their roles Pending Invitations — invitations that have been sent but not yet accepted

Select Invite User in the top-right corner. Enter the user's Email address. Select a Role from the dropdown. Select Send Invitation. The user will receive an invitation email. They appear under Pending Invitations until they accept and set up their account.
On the Pending Invitations tab, select the ⋯ menu on an invitation row to: Resend the invitation Delete the invitation

On the Users tab, select the ⋯ menu on the user's row. Select Edit Role to assign a different role, or Remove to revoke their access. warning Removing a user immediately revokes their access to Dalgo. They will not be able to log in until re-invited.

Can't find what you need in these docs? Here's how to get help.
Option When to use Getting help Reporting a bug, asking a question, or requesting a feature Troubleshooting Something isn't working — common issues and fixes

Email: support@dalgo.org Discord: Join the Dalgo server — fastest for quick questions GitHub: DalgoT4D on GitHub — report bugs or browse the codebase Blog: projecttech4dev.org — updates and case studies
The fastest way to get help is Discord. For bugs and account issues, email is best.

Join the Dalgo Discord server and post in the #dalgo-support channel. The Dalgo team and the community check it regularly. Best for: "How do I do X?" "Is this a bug or expected behaviour?" Questions while you're actively setting something up

Email support@dalgo.org for: Bugs that affect your data or pipelines Billing and subscription changes Account access issues (locked out, invitation not received) Requesting a new feature When reporting a bug, include: What you were trying to do What happened instead Any error message you saw (a screenshot helps) The approximate time it happened
Browse or open issues at github.com/DalgoT4D. If you're technical and want to contribute a fix or feature, pull requests are welcome. Related: Troubleshooting
Common issues and how to fix them.

Go to Data → Overview and find the failed pipeline. Select the red bar to see which task failed. Expand the task to read its logs — the error message usually says what went wrong. Common causes: Source credentials expired — re-enter credentials in Data → Ingest → Sources dbt model error — open Data → Transform → DBT Transform and run the failing task manually to see the full error Warehouse connection dropped — test the connection in Data → Ingest → Warehouse

Check when your last pipeline ran in Data → Overview. If the pipeline hasn't run recently, trigger it manually from Data → Orchestrate. If the pipeline is running successfully but charts still look stale, the dashboard may be drawing from a different table than expected. Contact support@dalgo.org.

Check that the credentials you entered are correct. Make sure Dalgo's IP addresses are whitelisted in the source system's firewall: 13.202.128.47 65.2.173.97 Try Save Changes and Test again. If the test fails, the error message will say whether it's a credential or network issue.

Check your spam folder for an email from notifications@dalgo.org. If it's not there, ask an Account Manager in your organisation to resend the invitation from Settings → User Management → Pending Invitations.

documents.t: Your implementation partner or the Dalgo team needs to set up pipelines for your organisation first. Reach out to them or contact support@dalgo.org.
